articles.headline: ASP.NET Identity: practical authentication and authorization
articles.summary: A concise guide to shipping secure sign-in, roles, and policies without over-engineering your auth layer.

content.text: ASP.NET Identity is still the fastest way to get a secure auth surface shipped. When the business needs email/password, external login providers, and role-based access today, the built-in primitives reduce risk and let you focus on product.
Identity gives you token hygiene, password hashing, and security stamps out of the box. The win is consistency: every project gets the same safe defaults.

items: Add Identity to the DI container and configure password rules.
Register cookie settings and lockout defaults.
Use policies instead of hard-coded role checks in controllers.
Skipping email confirmation for privileged accounts.
Not rotating refresh tokens on sensitive actions.

content.text: Security is not a feature you bolt on. It is a habit you repeat in every endpoint.

articles.headline: Designing a DbContext that scales with your app
articles.summary: Keep your Entity Framework Core layer predictable, testable, and ready for growth with a few structural decisions.

content.text: A DbContext should reflect your domain boundaries, not every table in the database. The more intentional the shape, the easier it is to evolve your system.
Group aggregates that change together. Map join tables through explicit entities if you need auditability or metadata.

items: Use scoped lifetime for web apps to keep tracking contained.
Use separate contexts for read models to avoid tracking overhead.
Avoid leaking DbContext outside the service layer.

content.text: Name migrations descriptively and review SQL diffs. It is easier to keep data safe when migrations are treated as production artifacts.

articles.headline: Shipping Semantic Kernel features without surprises
articles.summary: A pragmatic checklist for putting AI workflows into production while keeping costs and latency under control.

content.text: When AI features go live, reliability matters more than raw capability. A stable prompt pipeline beats a brilliant demo every time.
Log prompts, latency, and token usage per request. This makes regressions visible and keeps spend predictable.

items: Validate inputs before hitting the model.
Add safety filters on the output for PII and policy violations.
Cache stable responses to reduce latency.

content.text: Aim for sub-2 second p95 by caching, chunking, and precomputing embeddings. The user experience should never stall on a cold model response.
